reviews.summary: This paper presents Assign-and-Verify, a training-free compositional text-to-image reranking method that combines detector boxes, DAAM attention traces, explicit slot assignment with null nodes, and crop-based SigLIP verification. The authors report a negative pilot result: on 30 prompt-seed decisions, their DAAM-assisted method (0.5333 all-atoms-pass) underperforms simpler detector-only (0.6000) and crop-structured baselines (0.6000), with the strongest ablation removing explicit null/count handling (0.8000).
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 8+ distinct online searches. The key related works exist: LogicRank (Deiseroth et al., 2022) for logic-based compositional reranking, Miranda et al. (2025) for crop-structured inference at test time, T2I-FineEval and CompAlign for atomic region-aware evaluation, and DAAM (Tang et al., 2023 ACL Best Paper) for diffusion attention analysis. The specific combination of DAAM + explicit slot assignment with null nodes + counterfactual SigLIP scoring is novel. However, the core components are well-established. The paper correctly positions itself as an incremental analysis rather than claiming major methodological novelty. The negative result itself has some novelty value—it challenges the assumption that DAAM traces improve assignment.
strengths: Honest reporting of negative pilot results. The paper does not oversell successes and explicitly states that the proposed method underperforms simpler baselines.
Complete implementation with well-structured code (exp/shared/rerank.py shows proper implementation of assignment, scoring, and aggregation).
Sound methodology combining multiple established techniques (GroundingDINO, DAAM, SigLIP) in a principled way.
Useful ablations showing that null/count handling is the bottleneck (improving from 0.5333 to 0.8000 when removed).
All results numbers in the paper match exactly with results.json (verified Tables 1 and 2 against the JSON).
References are all real and verified (LogicRank, DAAM, GenEval, T2I-CompBench, etc. all exist with correct authors and venues).
The paper acknowledges limitations explicitly (small pilot scale, missing manual annotations, skipped official benchmark evaluation).
weaknesses: Extremely small experimental scope: only 30 prompt-seed decisions (10 prompts × 3 seeds) for the main results. This is insufficient for statistical confidence.
Missing planned mechanism metrics: manual grounding annotation was skipped, so the core claim about assignment accuracy cannot be verified.
The title in the LaTeX file is incorrect ('Winner-Take-All Low-Rank Adaptation' instead of the paper's actual title).
The paper does not achieve its stated goal of showing DAAM improves assignment—the evidence goes the other way (removing DAAM improves performance).
No official GenEval or T2I-CompBench metrics reported; only internal verifier metrics are used.
Single-run results without confidence intervals or proper statistical testing between methods (though McNemar test code exists in analyze/run.py).
The 'all-atoms-pass' metric is a strict binary threshold which may be noisy with only 30 samples.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is a solid pilot study with honest negative results, which is valuable for the community. However, the scope is too limited for acceptance as a full conference paper. To strengthen: (1) Complete the manual grounding annotation to verify whether assignment accuracy actually correlates with reranking performance—this is the core mechanism claim. (2) Expand evaluation to the full planned 200 prompts with official GenEval/T2I-CompBench metrics. (3) Debug the null/count scoring—the ablation showing 0.8000 vs 0.5333 suggests the current implementation penalizes correct assignments. (4) Fix the LaTeX title. (5) Add statistical significance tests. The framing should emphasize what was learned about assignment calibration rather than claiming DAAM benefits. The code quality is good and would support reproducibility with expanded experiments.
questions_for_authors: The ablation removing explicit null/count handling improves performance dramatically (0.5333 → 0.8000). Have you analyzed whether the count_score formula or the force_non_null threshold (-0.25) is miscalibrated?
Without manual grounding labels, how can you claim the mechanism is 'assignment' rather than some other aspect of the pipeline?
Why was the planned 200-prompt evaluation not completed? Was it a time constraint or a technical blocker?
Have you considered using DAAM in a different way—e.g., for filtering false positive detector boxes rather than as a direct assignment cue?
reviews.integrity_check: Results appear genuine. The experiment code exists and implements the described method (verified exp/shared/rerank.py and exp/assign_and_verify/run.py). The results.json numbers match exactly with Tables 1 and 2 in the paper (e.g., assign_and_verify 0.5333, detector_only_structured 0.6000, ablation_null_and_count 0.8000). Logs show actual execution with realistic runtimes (~0.6-22s per image). No evidence of fabricated data. All references verified as real publications. One minor issue: the LaTeX title macro appears to be incorrect (winner-take-all LoRA instead of Assign-and-Verify).

reviews.summary: The paper studies a narrowly framed, training-free reranking pipeline for compositional text-to-image generation under a fixed SDXL best-of-4 cache. It combines prompt parsing, detector boxes, cached DAAM attention mass, crop-based SigLIP verification, and relation geometry to test whether explicit slot assignment improves reranking, and presents the current evidence as a negative pilot result.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks with multiple search strategies: exact-title searches for the submission title found no obvious prior match; exact-title searches for cited nearest neighbors retrieved LogicRank (arXiv:2208.13518, https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.13518), Adding simple structure at inference improves Vision-Language Compositionality (arXiv:2506.09691, https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.09691), T2I-FineEval (arXiv:2503.11481, https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.11481), and CompAlign (arXiv:2505.11178, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.11178); component searches surfaced Training-Free Structured Diffusion Guidance for Compositional Text-to-Image Synthesis (arXiv:2212.05032, https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.05032), Compositional Text-to-Image Synthesis with Attention Map Control of Diffusion Models (arXiv:2305.13921, https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13921), Improving Compositional Text-to-image Generation with Large Vision-Language Models (arXiv:2310.06311, https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06311), and InfSplign (arXiv:2512.17851, https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.17851). Taken together, prior work already covers logic-based reranking, crop/segment-structured inference, fine-grained compositional evaluation, and attention-based grounding/control. The paper's claimed contribution is therefore at best an incremental recombination around explicit assignment analysis, and the implementation does not provide the promised assignment-accuracy evidence needed to make that incremental step compelling.
strengths: The paper is unusually honest about a negative pilot outcome and does not claim an unsupported win for the DAAM-assisted method.
The methodological question is well scoped: isolating assignment as the variable of interest is cleaner than presenting another broad compositional verifier.
The writing is generally clear, and the main comparison/ablation tables are easy to read and interpret at face value.
Experiment code is present in the workspace, and the core reranking pipeline is implemented rather than merely described.
weaknesses: The main integrity check fails: the numbers reported in the paper and top-level results.json are not backed by the current raw per-method result files. The paper reports 30-row pilot means such as detector-only/crop-structured = 0.6000 and full method = 0.5333, but the live raw files results/detector_only_structured.jsonl, results/crop_structured_siglip.jsonl, results/daam_no_counterfactual.jsonl, and results/assign_and_verify.jsonl currently contain only 3 rows each, with materially different means (0.6667, 0.3333, 0.0000, 0.0000 respectively).

Timestamps show staleness/synchronization problems: results.json and artifacts/tables/main_results.csv were written at 03:42 on 2026-03-21, while several raw method files were rewritten later around 03:51-03:52. This directly undermines trust in the paper tables and figures.
Because the reported table values do not match the current raw outputs, this triggers the guideline's automatic rejection condition for results integrity.

The code does not match the paper's strongest methodological wording. The manuscript describes assignment as a capacitated matching problem, but exp/shared/rerank.py implements a simple greedy per-group box selection with null thresholding, not a principled matching solver.
The key mechanism metric is absent. Manual grounding/assignment evaluation is explicitly skipped, so the paper cannot test its central claim that DAAM helps via better assignment.

Experimental scope is far below the preregistered plan: no completed 200-prompt test study, no completed transfer study in the surfaced results, no official GenEval/T2I-CompBench evaluation, and no meaningful statistical testing in the paper.
Novelty is weak relative to nearby work: LogicRank, Miranda et al., T2I-FineEval, CompAlign, StructureDiffusion, and attention-control methods already occupy most of the conceptual space.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The first priority is to repair integrity, not polish the narrative. Regenerate all aggregates, tables, and figures from the current raw outputs and ensure the paper matches them exactly; as submitted, the manuscript appears to rely on stale summaries. After that, align the method description with the code: either implement the claimed capacitated assignment solver or rewrite the paper to describe the actual greedy assignment used. To support the novelty claim, the authors need the missing mechanism evidence: manual assignment labels or another direct assignment-accuracy benchmark showing that DAAM changes assignment quality, not just final reranking. Finally, the work should be reframed as a small empirical systems/pilot study unless the full registered evaluation is completed, including official benchmark metrics, synchronized logs, and proper paired statistical analysis.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide a synchronized artifact bundle in which paper tables, results.json, artifacts/tables/main_results.csv, and all results/*.jsonl files are regenerated from the same run state?
Why were the raw method files for several baselines overwritten after the aggregate summaries were produced, and which set of outputs should reviewers trust?
Can you supply the missing assignment-accuracy annotations or another direct mechanism metric that actually tests the DAAM-assisted assignment hypothesis?
If the intended method is a capacitated matching formulation, why does the released implementation use greedy assignment in exp/shared/rerank.py instead?
Can you provide official GenEval/T2I-CompBench evaluations and the full planned split coverage, or else explicitly downgrade the paper to a pilot report?
reviews.integrity_check: References appear mostly real and verifiable from online sources, including LogicRank (https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.13518), DAAM (https://aclanthology.org/2023.acl-long.310.pdf), GenEval (NeurIPS D&B 2023), T2I-CompBench (NeurIPS D&B 2023), T2I-FineEval (https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.11481), CompAlign (https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.11178), Miranda et al. (https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.09691), and InfSplign (https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.17851). The fatal issue is results integrity: the paper's reported main-table numbers match stale top-level summaries but not the current raw experiment files or exp/*/results.json. Logs and timestamps indicate the aggregate summaries predate later rewrites of the raw method outputs, so the presented quantitative evidence is not currently trustworthy.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Assign-and-Verify, a training-free compositional text-to-image reranker that combines GroundingDINO detector boxes, cached DAAM cross-attention traces, explicit slot-to-box assignment with null nodes, crop-based SigLIP verification, and soft conjunction aggregation. The paper honestly reports a negative pilot result: on 30 prompt-seed decisions (10 prompts × 3 seeds), the DAAM-assisted method (0.5333 all-atoms-pass) underperforms simpler detector-only structured baselines (0.6000), and the strongest ablation (removing null/count handling) reaches 0.8000, suggesting assignment calibration rather than grounding signal quality is the bottleneck.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches confirmed: (a) the exact paper title appears nowhere in the literature; (b) no prior work uses DAAM cross-attention traces as an auxiliary signal for post-hoc compositional reranking or slot assignment; (c) the explicit formulation of reranking as a slot-assignment problem with null nodes is novel. However, the individual components are well-established: atomic compositional decomposition (GenEval, T2I-CompBench, T2I-FineEval, CompAlign), crop-based CLIP/SigLIP scoring (Miranda et al. 2025), detector-based region proposals (LogicRank, GenEval), geometric relation verification from bounding boxes, and geometric mean aggregation are all standard. The novelty is modest—it lies in the specific combination and the assignment-centric framing, not in any individual component. The negative result, while honest, limits the contribution since it shows the novel element (DAAM-assisted assignment) does not actually help.
strengths: Unusually honest reporting of negative results: the paper clearly states the proposed DAAM-assisted method underperforms simpler baselines and does not try to spin the findings
Well-motivated research question: isolating slot-to-region assignment as the key variable in compositional reranking is a clean and testable hypothesis
Informative ablation study: the finding that removing null/count handling improves all-atoms-pass from 0.5333 to 0.8000 provides a clear diagnostic of where the method fails and what to fix

Clean, well-organized code: the experiment code in exp/ is modular (shared/rerank.py, shared/run_core.py, per-method run.py scripts) and implements exactly what the paper describes—the equations in the paper correspond directly to functions in the code
All 11 references are verified as real published papers with correct titles, authors, and venues—no fabricated citations
Good writing quality: the paper is clearly structured, the contributions are explicitly stated, and limitations are thoroughly discussed in Section 5
weaknesses: Fatally insufficient experimental scale: the entire study covers only 10 prompts × 3 seeds = 30 prompt-seed decisions from a 40-prompt dev split. This is far too small to draw any reliable conclusions about method performance, and the high standard deviations (±0.49–0.51 on a 0–1 metric) confirm the estimates are extremely noisy

No standard benchmark evaluation: GenEval and T2I-CompBench official metrics were not run, as the paper itself acknowledges. The reported 'all-atoms-pass' and 'final score' are internal verifier metrics with unknown correlation to established evaluation protocols

Missing primary mechanism evaluation: the paper's central thesis is about assignment quality, but no human grounding labels were collected, so slot-assignment accuracy—the key quantity of interest—is never measured

The proposed method does not work: DAAM-assisted assignment (0.5333) performs worse than detector-only (0.6000). While honesty is appreciated, a negative result on 30 data points with no standard benchmarks provides limited scientific value

No statistical significance testing: with n=30 binary observations, the difference between 0.5333 and 0.6000 (2 out of 30 decisions) is almost certainly not statistically significant, yet the paper draws conclusions from it

Some result file inconsistencies: the main method JSONL files (assign_and_verify.jsonl, detector_only_structured.jsonl, etc.) contain only 3 rows each despite results.json claiming 30 rows—the full 30-row data exists only in the ablation files, suggesting partial overwrites from a later test run

Best-of-4 global SigLIP achieves exactly 0.0000 all-atoms-pass across all 30 decisions, which seems anomalously low and raises questions about whether the structured verifier metric is well-calibrated or meaningful

Detector-only structured and crop-structured SigLIP produce identical all-atoms-pass (0.6000) and final score (0.6526) despite being different methods, suggesting the evaluation may lack discriminative power at this scale
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a well-formulated research question—whether explicit slot assignment with DAAM improves compositional reranking—but the experimental evidence is far too thin to support any conclusion, positive or negative. The immediate priority for revision is scale: the 10-prompt pilot must be expanded to at least the full 40-prompt dev set and ideally to the planned 200-prompt test set with official GenEval/T2I-CompBench evaluation. Without standard benchmark numbers, the community cannot contextualize the results. Second, the missing human grounding annotation is critical: the paper's thesis is about assignment quality, so measuring slot-assignment accuracy against human labels is not optional but essential. Third, statistical rigor must be added—with binary outcomes on 30 samples, confidence intervals and significance tests (e.g., McNemar's test for paired comparisons) are needed to determine whether observed differences are real. Fourth, the anomalous behavior of global SigLIP (0.000 all-atoms-pass) and the identical performance of two different structured baselines should be investigated and explained—these patterns suggest the internal verifier may have calibration issues beyond the null/count problem identified in ablations. Finally, the result file inconsistencies (3 vs 30 rows in main method files) should be cleaned up to ensure full reproducibility. The paper's honest framing and clear writing are assets that should be preserved in revision.
questions_for_authors: Why do detector-only structured and crop-structured SigLIP produce identical all-atoms-pass and final scores despite using different assignment sources? Does this indicate that assignment source doesn't matter for these prompts, or that the evaluation metric lacks sensitivity?

The best-of-4 global SigLIP achieves 0.000 all-atoms-pass across all 30 decisions. Is this because SigLIP-selected images systematically fail the structured verifier, or because the structured verifier is miscalibrated? Have you inspected the selected images qualitatively?
The main method JSONL files contain only 3 rows (1 prompt × 3 seeds) while results.json reports metrics over 30 rows. Can you clarify which data the summary metrics were computed from?

Given that removing null/count handling improves all-atoms-pass to 0.8000, have you considered that the count verification formula exp(-|m-c|)·exp(-0.5·extra) may be too harsh—for instance, penalizing extra detections that may be false positives from GroundingDINO?
What would it take to run the planned GenEval official evaluation? Is it a matter of compute, implementation, or something else? This seems critical for a revision.
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is adequate but has minor concerns. All numbers in Tables 2 and 3 match results.json exactly to full floating-point precision. The experiment code in exp/shared/rerank.py implements the equations described in the paper (compatibility scoring, count scoring, attribute scoring with counterfactual negatives, relation scoring, geometric mean aggregation). Figures match the numerical data. Per-row result JSONL files contain detailed slot assignments with bounding box coordinates, confidence scores, and DAAM mass values consistent with actual model execution. However, the main method JSONL files (assign_and_verify.jsonl, detector_only_structured.jsonl, crop_structured_siglip.jsonl, daam_no_counterfactual.jsonl) contain only 3 rows each despite the summary claiming 30 rows—the full data appears to be preserved in the ablation JSONL files. This is a bookkeeping issue rather than fabrication, but it undermines reproducibility. All 11 cited references are verified as real published papers.

aggregated_feedback:
--- Review 1 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 6) ---
  Weaknesses: Extremely small experimental scope: only 30 prompt-seed decisions (10 prompts × 3 seeds) for the main results. This is insufficient for statistical confidence.
Missing planned mechanism metrics: manual grounding annotation was skipped, so the core claim about assignment accuracy cannot be verified.
The title in the LaTeX file is incorrect ('Winner-Take-All Low-Rank Adaptation' instead of the paper's actual title).
The paper does not achieve its stated goal of showing DAAM improves assignment—the evidence goes the other way (removing DAAM improves performance).
No official GenEval or T2I-CompBench metrics reported; only internal verifier metrics are used.
Single-run results without confidence intervals or proper statistical testing between methods (though McNemar test code exists in analyze/run.py).
The 'all-atoms-pass' metric is a strict binary threshold which may be noisy with only 30 samples.
  Feedback: The paper is a solid pilot study with honest negative results, which is valuable for the community. However, the scope is too limited for acceptance as a full conference paper. To strengthen: (1) Complete the manual grounding annotation to verify whether assignment accuracy actually correlates with reranking performance—this is the core mechanism claim. (2) Expand evaluation to the full planned 200 prompts with official GenEval/T2I-CompBench metrics. (3) Debug the null/count scoring—the ablation showing 0.8000 vs 0.5333 suggests the current implementation penalizes correct assignments. (4) Fix the LaTeX title. (5) Add statistical significance tests. The framing should emphasize what was learned about assignment calibration rather than claiming DAAM benefits. The code quality is good and would support reproducibility with expanded experiments.
  Integrity: Results appear genuine. The experiment code exists and implements the described method (verified exp/shared/rerank.py and exp/assign_and_verify/run.py). The results.json numbers match exactly with Tables 1 and 2 in the paper (e.g., assign_and_verify 0.5333, detector_only_structured 0.6000, ablation_null_and_count 0.8000). Logs show actual execution with realistic runtimes (~0.6-22s per image). No evidence of fabricated data. All references verified as real publications. One minor issue: the LaTeX title macro appears to be incorrect (winner-take-all LoRA instead of Assign-and-Verify).
